title: Stacks & Queues
description: Implement stacks and queues with arrays in C — the fundamental data structures for DFS, BFS, and bracket-matching interview problems.

sections.title: Array-based stack
sections.explanation: A stack is LIFO (Last In, First Out). In C, implement with an array and a `top` index initialized to -1. **Push**: `arr[++top] = val`. **Pop**: `return arr[top--]`. **Peek**: `arr[top]`. **Empty**: `top == -1`. This is the standard C interview stack.
sections.example:
int stack[100];
int top = -1;

stack[++top] = 10;  // push 10
stack[++top] = 20;  // push 20
stack[++top] = 30;  // push 30

printf("%d\n", stack[top]);     // peek: 30
printf("%d\n", stack[top--]);   // pop: 30
printf("%d\n", stack[top]);     // peek: 20

exercise.prompt: Implement a stack using an array. Push values 5, 10, 15. Pop once and print the popped value. Then print the current top of stack.

validation.message: Pop returns 15 (last pushed), top is now 10
Use ++top for push and top-- for pop with initial top = -1

exercise.hint: Push with stack[++top] = val. Pop with stack[top--]. After popping 15, top points to 10.

sections.title: Array-based queue
sections.explanation: A queue is FIFO (First In, First Out). Implement with an array and two indices: `front` and `back`. **Enqueue**: `arr[back++] = val`. **Dequeue**: `return arr[front++]`. **Empty**: `front == back`. For interview purposes, a simple linear queue with enough space works fine.
sections.example:
int queue[100];
int front = 0, back = 0;

queue[back++] = 10;  // enqueue 10
queue[back++] = 20;  // enqueue 20
queue[back++] = 30;  // enqueue 30

printf("%d\n", queue[front++]);  // dequeue: 10
printf("%d\n", queue[front]);    // peek: 20

exercise.prompt: Implement a queue using an array. Enqueue 100, 200, 300. Dequeue twice, printing each dequeued value. Then print the remaining front element.

validation.message: Dequeue gives 100, then 200, then front is 300
Use back++ for enqueue and front++ for dequeue

exercise.hint: Enqueue with queue[back++]. Dequeue with queue[front++]. FIFO: first in, first out.

sections.title: Valid parentheses with stack
sections.explanation: Classic interview problem: check if a string of brackets is valid using a stack. Push opening brackets, pop on closing brackets and check for match. If the stack is empty at the end and all matches were correct, the string is valid.

exercise.prompt: Implement the valid parentheses checker. Test it with "({[]})" (valid) and "([)]" (invalid). Print "valid" or "invalid" for each.

validation.message: Use a char stack to match opening and closing brackets

exercise.hint: Push opening brackets. On closing, pop and verify the match. Return top == -1 at the end.

sections.title: Reversing with a stack
sections.explanation: Stacks naturally reverse order — push all elements, then pop them all. This pattern is useful for reversing arrays, strings, or processing elements in reverse order without an extra reverse step.

exercise.prompt: Use a stack to print the array {10, 20, 30, 40, 50} in reverse order, space-separated.

validation.message: Push all elements then pop all to reverse

exercise.hint: Push all elements onto the stack, then pop them one by one to get reverse order.
